speaker: interviewer
text: Can we do a voice slate again?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do we do that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, they seem to.

speaker: interviewee
text: Is my voice shaking?

speaker: interviewer
text: No. I said a voice slate. I'm going to say who-- what this interview is. I guess that's in case, God forbid-- This is tape two, interview with Gloria Ungar, January 18, 1995 in Los Angeles. So now you've been taken with-- who were you with on the train to Sered?

speaker: interviewee
text: People. I don't know who.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your family, I mean.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you alone?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was alone with my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just your little brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My little brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you now? Tell me again.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember whether that was 1942 or 1943. I believe it must have been 1943. So I was-- I was 12 years old. I wasn't quite 13 yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your little brother--

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was a year and a half younger than me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was 11 and 1/2, give or take.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you got to Sered.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were let off the train. Do you remember what happened when you were let off the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were taken to the-- to the barracks. We were taken to the barracks. And after a time-- I don't remember how long, whether it was a day-- after a day, they made everybody come out in front-- in front of their own barracks. And we had to wait in line-- we had to wait there in line, and we were counted. And every barrack was separately put on a-- I believe they were not cars, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Trucks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Trucks, yeah. We were put on trucks, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Each barrack went into one truck.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. They were trucks. And I was with my little brother, and I heard that my father was in the next barrack. Somebody told me that they saw my father in the other barrack. And I was desperate to go with my father and with my older brother on the trains. We knew we were going to be shipped on the trains. We didn't know where, but we were going to be shipped.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was this after you had arrived? How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. Maybe two days. Not much longer, yeah. So I was desperate to, to be reunited with my father, with my brother. And I just-- as I was standing outside, I took my brother by the hand, and I stepped out of line and I went over to a SS guard.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I told him, please, I am an orphan, and I have my little brother. Would you please let me go with my father? I was shaking. I was scared. I was-- I knew that he could just take out his pistol and shoot me right there and then, but I figured, well, what's the difference, you know? Now or later or whatever. I just want to be with my father and my brother. And he took one look at me and he went with his hand like what the heck? You gonna be dead anyway, so go ahead. You go over there to the other side, you know? And that's how I was reunited with my father and with my brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were put in the cattle trains. And the only thing-- I mean, the only thing. I remember when we were in the cattle trains. We were like sardines, packed like sardines. And there was only little window there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I just remember my father crying. He says, what-- why are they doing this to my innocent children? I mean, they haven't lived anything. They haven't done anything. He says, at least myself, I had some sort of a life. I mean, he was probably 40 years old, maybe 43 years old. But you children, why? He just-- he was just crying bitterly.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he tells my older brother, he says, Jack, I want you to try to jump through that little, little window. When it's night time-- even though the guards were, you know, they were in trains watching everything-- I want you to try to jump out the window and save yourself so at least someone will survive and the family name will go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was willing to do it, you know? He figured, you know, I either die this way or that way. At least I'll try to save myself. But when the people heard in the train, in the wagon, what he intends to do, they got all very upset. And they said no, we will not allow that because we were counted and we were warned that if anybody escapes, then everybody will be shoot-- will be shot. And that was the end of it. So he couldn't-- he didn't even try anymore. I remember coming to Auschwitz. Everything is like in a daze, you know? I remember we arrived in the trains. They didn't let us out right away. When they opened up the doors from the cattle trains, it was like a pandemonium. It was just-- it's just like a-- like a nightmare, you know? Everything was in a daze. Was it day or night? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was night. It was black. It was night. And the SS guards were screaming, [GERMAN]. Out, out. Everybody out. They were standing there with the dogs and with the [GERMAN]. And children were crying. Old people were-- they were old and sick. And there were parents holding onto their children, to their grandchildren. It was just a pandemonium. It was just-- the air was foul. I mean, it just smelled. The sky was red. We didn't know what it was, but it was from burning of the bodies. The sky was red, absolutely red. I never saw anything like it in my life. And I didn't have a mother. So I was a little girl. They said women to one side, men to the other side. Well, I didn't belong to the women. I was a little girl. So my father was holding onto me.

speaker: interviewee
text: All of a sudden, a lady comes to my father and she says to him, what are you doing? You give me your child. You give me your daughter. What kind of woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know who she is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she a prisoner?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Another prisoner?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. She was also one with somebody who came on the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean on the train with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Evidently, she knew my father. I don't remember who she was, and I never saw her again. And she grabbed me from my father's hand, and she took me over to the women's side. And, and it was night and Mengele was there. And he was just-- he didn't ask you how old you are. That was-- you know. He just said right, left, right, left, right, left. And evidently. it was neither. He didn't see that I was so young, so he pushed me to the right side and that's how I didn't go to the gas chambers. My youngest brother was with my father and with my oldest brother, with Jack. And yeah, Nathan was with Jack.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Mengele asked him, is this your brother? Is this your son? And he says, no. This is my brother. And right away, he pushed him over to the gas chamber side. And we never saw him again.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father and my brother were sent to work in separate camps. They didn't keep them together. They separated the families as much as they could. I was in Auschwitz and right away, they, they shaved our heads. And they were sending us to take showers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we knew already what showers means. Showers means gas. How did you know this?

speaker: interviewee
text: We already knew. We had already information in Slovakia what they are doing in Auschwitz. So we knew that-- we knew that probably it's we are going to the gas chambers. However, when we got to the showers, I was thinking to myself it couldn't be the gas chamber. Why would they shave us? And why would the-- these are only young women. I mean, they wouldn't separate us if they were going to send us to the gas chambers because to the gas chambers they sent everybody, you know-- the young, the old. So after the showers, we survived. We saw it was water. It was not gas. And they gave us those striped, striped dresses, you know. And they put us in, in barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you a number?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I have my number here. I have my number.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: They tattooed it with a needle.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember if it hurt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. It hurt. But it wasn't the worst thing, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did that? Who did the tattooing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Women.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Jewish women. Inmates. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know? I mean they just-- you just went there and they said, give us your arm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just went there and they just--

speaker: interviewee
text: They knew the number.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. They gave you the number.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there a lot of people doing it at one time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were standing in the rows, you know? One after the other. One after the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was just one woman doing it, or a group of women?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think there were two women there. Just two women.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take? Do you remember? Several minutes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too long. The tattooing didn't last too long.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it bleed? Did your arm bleed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. And when--

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you-- I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get shoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We got those Holland shoes, you know? Those wooden shoes. And I tell you, that was a nightmare, these shoes because they made us go to work. Well, they made us get up early in the morning and the weather was very cold already. And all we had is those wooden shoes and that striped uniform.

speaker: interviewer
text: No underwear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. No sweater. No jackets. No nothing. We had to stand outside, five girls to a row. And they had to count us because they were afraid maybe somebody disappeared someplace. I don't know where. The only way to disappear was, was through death. There was no way to disappear from there. And we just had to stand while they counted us. And if somebody fell or died or whatever, you just had to leave them there. You could not move. You could not do anything, or they would shoot you if you helped anybody. And that took hours because sometimes the count wasn't right. It wasn't just us. They counted all the camps, and they had to tally everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which Lager were you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the B Lager.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was doing the counting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who was doing the counting? The Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: The German women. And they were the worst kind. They were worse than the men.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anyone in particular?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I just remember that the women were very cruel. They, they, they had no pity on anybody. Sometimes, you would see maybe a man would have a pity on somebody and just would look away, you know? But the women were very, you know, very bad. They were very cruel. And after that, we got-- after the counting, we would each get a slice of black bread and a cup of black water which was supposed to be coffee, but it was made from chicoria. There was no coffee at that time. And that was-- that was your food for the day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember where you got the food? Was it inside? Did you go back in the barracks to get the food?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went back to the barracks, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of year was this when you got to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got to Auschwitz in September in 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So it was not too cold yet?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was cold already.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was cold.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was already cold. It's fall there, and it was very cold already. And it was raining, and it was muddy. And I remember they took us to work. And we had to go through the gates where the music was playing, you know. And you just had to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that. Tell me about the music.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was unbelievable. There was an orchestra just playing there. And they were all inmates from the concentration camp. And you just had to walk through and not make a face or nothing. You just had to march to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of music would they play?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall.

speaker: interviewer
text: March music or classical?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so. I can't recall. I just remember there was music. And they made us march, march. And what we did was we had to-- they sent us to a quarry to get the rocks which they needed for paving streets, you know? And it was very hard work. And, and it was muddy. And you had to pull your shoes out with your hands, those wooden shoes. They just-- you know, it was very hard to walk in them.

speaker: interviewer
text: You would fall in the mud? It would sink into the mud--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Right. You had to use your hands to pull them out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of tools did you have to work with in the quarry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Picks. Axes. These--

speaker: interviewer
text: Wheelbarrows.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wheelbarrows, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were chipping away at rock.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with anybody? Did you know anybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: All by yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was all by myself. All by myself. And I developed sores, all-- sores, which then I found out that they are vitamin-- from vitamin deficiency. And somebody told me that there is an infirmary there and there is a doctor there. I should go there. But if you go there and you stay overnight there, in the morning they come. They take you away and they take you to the gas chamber. So I didn't dare to go there. I wanted to live.

speaker: interviewee
text: So somebody, who she said she was a nurse, she just took a knife and she opened these wounds and she just let the pus out. And I just went on to work. Where were the sores?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Auschwitz, in the barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: On your hands?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Part of my body, over on my legs and, you know, all over.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did she open it?

speaker: interviewee
text: With a knife. She just cut it open and she just let it drain, and just went on. And in Auschwitz probably, you couldn't survive too long like that because the winter was coming. And anyway, we had no control over what they would do with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they put us on trains again. The Russians were coming closer, so they wanted to empty out Auschwitz. And they put us on cattle trains again, and we ended up in Bergen-Belsen. Now, when would this have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember being in Bergen-Belsen Christmastime.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were probably in Auschwitz--

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, all the days were all-- everything was-- I mean, I couldn't tell the days. Everything was the same. But I remember that it was Christmas because I remember the guards, you know, celebrating Christmas.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in Auschwitz between September of 1944--

speaker: interviewee
text: And December.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you make any connections with any of the women? Did you have anybody that you could, as a young girl, turn to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody. I had nobody. When we got to Bergen-Belsen, it was-- this was not an extermination camp where they gassed you or put you in a crematoria, but it was-- they starved you. And you just died from starvation. They didn't make you work there.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was no work.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not a working camp. They did not make me work there. And remember, it was very, very cold. There was a lot of snow on the ground. And it was very cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a coat or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Still nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had no coat. It was very cold. And I was so weak and so hungry that I remember sitting in front of my barrack on, on, on, on the step. And there were people lying all over dead and sick and moaning. There was a lot of typhoid fever there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And people were just dying all over. There were just corpses lying all over. And I was so weak from starvation, I couldn't get up from the step. I just couldn't get up. And I knew that probably I'm going to die like the others there, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember a lady coming over to me. And she says to me, I'm coming from the other camp, from the other Lager in Bergen-Belsen-- because there were a few Lagers there. I just carried some cans of that chicoria water. They carried it from one camp to the other. And I just saw your Aunt Mina-- that's my mother's sister-- with her little child. She's there. Many relatives came-- just came in today because they did not ship them to Auschwitz anymore. The Russians are there, and there is no more Auschwitz, and that's why they shipped them to Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, can you imagine? They were losing the war. And that's all they had is on their minds how to kill all the Jews. That's all they did. They didn't worry about losing the war. That's all. They were just busy with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she says, your aunt is here, and she's sending you her first slice of bread because she just arrived and she's not so hungry. Here, you go ahead and eat it. And it-- I mean, just revived me. It revived me. And she says, I'm going to make arrangements that the next time, you carry these-- this water, the chicoria to the other camp and so you get to see your aunt. And she did. And when I saw my aunt and her little boy, I was just so happy. I saw my other relatives, my other cousins. They were also there. And I just begged her. I said, Aunt Mina, please make some arrangements.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe I can come over to your Lager and stay with you. I just-- I just didn't care if I died tomorrow as long as I am with someone from the family. And she advised me. She says, look, first, I don't know if I can arrange that, you know? How could-- how can I arrange it?

speaker: interviewee
text: And secondly, she says, I have a small child. They are not going to let us live. You know how that is. They're not going to let us live. They going to kill us anyway. You still have a chance. If you-- you know, they put you to work. Maybe you will survive. And if you do survive, remember that after the war, we will all meet in Betliar in Slovakia. If anybody survives, let's, let's meet there. And I had no choice. So then they shipped me again. They took us from Bergen-Belsen--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your whole Lager?

speaker: interviewee
text: My whole Lager, yes. They shipped us again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Again by cattle car?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also by cattle, yeah. And the next place I remember was Magdeburg. It was an ammunition factory in a salt mine. We had to go underground to a salt factory, which the walls were from salt. Everything was from salt. And that's where they were manufacturing ammunition parts. They made us work there. They had other prisoners there, too. They had Russian prisoners of war. They were working. And they had political prisoners. We were not supposed to mingle with them at all. We were separated.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work were you doing there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Parts. Some screws. I don't know. On machines. Just, just parts. We were not supposed to talk to anybody. We were treated differently. We did not get the food they got. We did not get the clothing that they got. We were completely separated. And at night, we were warehoused in, in barns, in horse barns.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like stables?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. In stables, yes. The floors were from cement. And they put just some straw on it. And that's how we slept there. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: --not everybody had a blanket, you know? Few people had to share.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how many people there were in the stable?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hundreds.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hundreds.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hundreds, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were all people who had been with you in Bergen-Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you knew some of these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew some of them, yes. I knew some of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So would you march, then, from the stable to the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a long--

speaker: interviewee
text: Every day, we marched. And you would think that-- you know, the Germans say that they never saw anything. Of course they saw. They saw us marching every day. I mean, they just closed their eyes. They didn't-- how can they say that they didn't know what was going on?

speaker: interviewer
text: In the stripes? In the striped uniform, still? In the striped uniform dress?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. Of course, in the striped uniform, sure. So that was in Magdeburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long had you been in Bergen-Belsen? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bergen-Belsen, I was maybe a month. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you took that-- then they took you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then from Magdeburg, they loaded us again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in Magdeburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. Month or weeks. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you worked there.

speaker: interviewee
text: We worked there, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you a little tiny bit stronger than you had been the day you sat on the steps of the barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was. Yes, I was. I wanted to live very much. I always wanted to survive. I don't know. I guess when you are young, you are hopeful, and you don't want to give up. And you always hope that things will work out.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- in the-- when you were in the stable, do you remember who you were next to, who you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Just young girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: --sharing a blanket.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just young girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, and most of them were young girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of them were young women. In Auschwitz also, we were in bunks. We had those barracks. And to every bed, I believe we were eight girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were no really adult women there? You were all just girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. They were all adult women.

speaker: interviewer
text: But teen--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were all adult women. Not young children, only adult women. I mean, I was the youngest. I didn't think anybody-- I mean, there were young women like, l guess, from 16 to maybe 40. Nobody older than that because 40 was considered old already.

speaker: interviewer
text: So basically, was it the same group that had been in Auschwitz and then went to Bergen-Belsen and now is in Magdeburg.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Right. Yeah. From Magdeburg, they put us again on cattle trains. And they took us to four other concentration camps. And I do not recall the names at all. I don't remember. We didn't work there. They just-- they were just-- I don't know why they were sending us from one place to the other. They just didn't want the Russians to get us. They were just sending us.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were-- was it the same SS-- these were German guard women.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Women who were moving you around.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Well, men. Men. The SS guard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it the same people moving you around all the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. They were always different kinds.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it also people from the-- from the country or from the towns, or was it just the SS people?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were just the SS people. They were the guards. They were on the trains, and they were with us all the time. Always different ones, but always there, you know, the SS guards.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long would you say you were in each place when you would come-- you were in four different camps. Were you there a couple of nights, a couple of weeks? What?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe three, four weeks. And then we were on again.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't work in those camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what you would do? Do you remember how your day-- I know you probably had the [NON-ENGLISH] work in the camp--

speaker: interviewee
text: What we did, we were talking about how it was at home. We were always hungry, so we were always cooking and reminiscing what delicious foods we had at home. And mostly we were talking about food. We were always hungry and cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you would talk about food, would you-- would you say what you would want to eat if you could--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh. One of these days, we're going to eat all these delicious things. And we were swapping recipes and talking about all the delicious food that one of these days, maybe we will still eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were-- at this point, were the women from a lot of different countries?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Most of them were Hungarian girls. Some of them were Slovakian. And I never told anybody that I came from Poland, that I was originally from Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why not?

speaker: interviewee
text: Somehow, they-- the Hungarians hated the Poles. The Poles hated the Hungarians. It's not nice to say that, but it's how it was. So I never told anybody that I came from Poland. I always said I came from Slovakia because my mother came from Slovakia and that's where I was, so I was among the Hungarian girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Looking back on it now as an adult, why do you think that there was this animosity between Jewish people from different towns?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't know. I really don't know. I guess it's prejudice, you know, like any other prejudice. I really don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you would mainly lie on the barracks.

speaker: interviewee
text: We would lie in the barracks. Or we would be forever counted. They always counted us, you know? And we'd lay in the barracks. And we just did nothing, you know, those couple of weeks, you know? Just waited till we get food again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's what I was going to ask you. Would they bring in food in the morning?

speaker: interviewee
text: Once a day, we got the food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Once a day only.

speaker: interviewee
text: Once a day, we got the bread and the black coffee.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was it, then? No hot food except for the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No hot food. Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the coffee even a little bit hot?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was supposed to be, yeah. I remember my cousin, when he came to Auschwitz and he didn't know what was going on. And they were giving out that coffee. And he says, no, I want from the other one. I want the tea.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that woman took and she slapped him on the face. She slapped him. She says, what do you think you are? You can't ask tea. [LAUGHTER] After that, he knew that he can't ask for anything. So you were-- you were in these camps. And like you said, you were just sort of waiting.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Until they sent-- ship us again. We didn't know where to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any idea what was going on in the outside world?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't. I didn't. Maybe I was too young. Maybe others did know maybe something, but not much. I certainly didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the barracks, who were the women there? I mean, who was in charge? Was it-- again, was it the Jewish administration?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the barracks, there was Jewish administration. They had-- they called them the kapos.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Let's talk about that. We're going to change tape, then we're going to talk about that.
